title: Vincent Lee
content:
{{Top|rwp}}
{{Person
|image=[[File:VincentLee.jpg]]
|name=Vincent Lee
|birth=c. [[1960s#1968|1968]]<ref>''[[Star Wars: Rebel Assault: The Official Insider's Guide]]'' mentions him as being 24 years old around the time he started working on the game.</ref>
|death=
|listedpronouns=<!--Only include pronouns if they have been explicitly specified by the individual to which this article pertains.-->

'''Vincent Lee''' was designer, programmer and project leader on the [[LucasArts]] game ''[[Star Wars: Rebel Assault]]'' and its sequel ''[[Star Wars: Rebel Assault II: The Hidden Empire]]''.

==Biography==
As a child, Vince Lee was first exposed to [[Star Wars video games|video game]]s after joining the [[Wikipedia:Cub Scout|Cub Scouts]]. On a field trip to the [[Wikipedia:Lawrence Hall of Science|Lawrence Hall of Science]], an interactive [[Wikipedia:Museum|museum]] in [[Wikipedia:Berkeley, California|Berkeley]], he had the opportunity to play [[Wikipedia:Computer Space|''Star Trek'']] and [[Wikipedia:Lunar Lander (arcade game)|''Lunar Lander'']], both text-based games played on a teletype which printed to rolls of yellow paper. Despite the primitive games on offer, the experience sparked Lee's interest in games. A few years later, his father assembled a [[Wikipedia:Zenith Z89|Heathkit H-89]] [[Wikipedia:Personal computer|personal computer]] from a kit, the family's first computer. With little [[Wikipedia:Software|software]] available, Lee learned to program in [[Wikipedia:BASIC|BASIC]] and [[Wikipedia:Assembly language|assembly language]] and began making games for it. At fifteen years old, he sold his first game.<ref name="LA">{{LucasArtsCite|url=products/rebel2/vl/vlframeset.htm|text=Vince Lee: Project Leader ''Rebel Assault II''}}</ref>

Lee later attended [[Wikipedia:College|college]] at the [[Wikipedia:University of California, Berkeley|University of California, Berkeley]], where he studied his [[Wikipedia:Bachelor's degree|Bachelor's degree]] in [[Wikipedia:Mechanical engineering|Mechanical Engineering]] for four years, before staying on for a year and a half to earn a [[Wikipedia:Master's degree|Master's degree]] in robotics and control systems. He spent much of his time programming, including work for a number of [[Wikipedia:Amiga|Amiga]] software developers, though he considered it a hobby.<ref name="LA" /><ref name="RA">'' [[Star Wars: Rebel Assault: The Official Insider's Guide]]''</ref>

Still as an undegraduated, at a User's Group meeting he met a LucasArts producer who left him his number.<ref name="RA" /> After leaving school, he was offered work at [[Wikipedia:IBM|IBM]] and [[Wikipedia:ExxonMobil|Exxon Production Research Houston]], but turned both down to take a job as a [[Wikipedia:Programmer|programmer]] for [[LucasArts|Lucasfilm Games]], which he started in [[Wikipedia:Spring (season)|Spring]] [[1991]].<ref name="LA" /><ref name="RA" />

Lee's early work involved developing tools on the personal computer and converting [[Wikipedia:SCUMM|SCUMM]] [[Wikipedia:Graphic adventure game|adventure games]] to the Amiga and [[Wikipedia:Sega CD|Sega CD]]. He started developing image-compression technology in [[January]] [[1992]]. He and other LucasArts colleagues attended the Winter [[1992|'92]] [[Wikipedia:Consumer Electronics Show|CES]] at Chicago and were exposed by the ambitious "multimedia" CD-ROM titles.<ref name="LA" /><ref name="RA" />

It was while he was working on the Amiga version of ''Indiana Jones and the Fate of Atlantis'' when the LucasArts GM suggested to work as a programmer for the CD-ROM title; the intimate knowledge of multimedia technology, as well as his "good gaming sense", eventually brought him to ''[[Star Wars: Rebel Assault]]'', what would be the company's first CD-ROM exclusive project.<ref name="RA" />

Lee developed [[SMUSH]], a custom tool that compressed the game data; eventually it was expanded to be a design and editing tool. This earned him the position of project leader and designer on ''Rebel Assault''.<ref name="LA" /><ref name="RA" /> He compiled the game missions and cutscenes as they were delivered from the artists, focusing also on game play elements, playability and smoothess, but he was getting involved in many directions simultaneously as well. Lee also was open to suggestions from Q/A, and had the final decision for their incorporation. Lead tester [[Brett Tosti]] commented that Lee's "programming genius" immediately detected a problem and could fix bugs when they were pointed out to him. Sometimes Lee was communicating with the support department of [[Wikipedia:Microsoft|Microsoft]], and after being told that something was not possible, in some minutes he could come up with a solution.<ref name="RA" /> 

Lee also did most of the sound work of the game, choosing portions of the original [[Musical score]] for each part of the game; he recorded his choices with a [[Wikipedia:boombox|boombox]], before discussing his preferences with [[Michael Land]] and his sound team.<ref name="RA" />

The whole process challenged Lee, who was busy with many things simultaneously and worked at nights and weekends, especially during the last months of post-production. [[Casey Donahue Ackley]] oversaw the last stages as Associate Producer, and facilitated communication with the art department. Lee and Ackley authored the game's manual and reference card.<ref name="RA" />
